lesson: Caching zobristKey results in a Map eliminates redundant fnv1a64 string hashing. High cache hit rate because most game state is unchanged between moves.

Trace/debug infrastructure (effectPath strings, withTracePath context spreads, resolveTraceProvenance objects, emitTrace argument construction) is a significant hidden cost when called 100K+ times. Skip ALL trace-related work when tracing is disabled — check the trace-enabled flag BEFORE doing any string construction, object creation, or context spreading.

V8 JIT optimizes object spreads, regex, and BigInt XOR efficiently. Micro-optimizations that try to avoid these patterns (Object.create(null), manual string parsing, split bigint pairs) are consistently SLOWER due to overhead exceeding savings or breaking V8 hidden classes.

Deep profiling before guessing prevents wasted experiments. The assumed bottleneck (setVar state spreads at 127ms) was dwarfed by the actual bottleneck (phase lifecycle effects at 17000ms, agent double-validation at 10000ms, listPlayers array allocation at 372K calls). ALWAYS profile first — add opt-in instrumentation to any depth.

Agent preparePlayableMoves was calling legalChoicesEvaluate + evaluatePlayableMoveCandidate (double-validation) for every legal move. Replacing with single-pass probeMoveViability eliminated 53% of agent time. Redundant validation across call boundaries is the #1 optimization target.

resolvePlayerSel called listPlayers (Array.from allocation) on EVERY call including the fast-path 'chosen' selector. With 372K pvar lookups, this created 372K unnecessary arrays. Fast-pathing the chosen selector with a bounds check instead saved 3.6%. Look for hidden allocations inside high-frequency selector/resolver functions.

V8 hidden class monomorphism is critical. Fast-paths that create objects with different property shapes at the same call site cause catastrophic deoptimization (17% regression in exp-026). Even different return points in the SAME function with the SAME property keys create polymorphism. Only optimize within the existing object creation pattern.

Functions called on every move (like dispatchTriggers) that are no-ops for certain game configurations (0 triggers) should fast-path before doing any work. The array copy [...triggerLog], resource creation, and graph resolution in dispatchTriggers were ~630ms of wasted work across 50 games.

advanceToDecisionPoint called legalMoves() just to check .length > 0, enumerating ALL legal moves across all actions. Adding earlyExitAfterFirst option stops after finding the first legal move, saving 3.39%. Pattern: any existence check that calls an enumerate-all function should use an early-exit variant.

computeAlwaysCompleteActionIds excluded parameterized actions, forcing probeMoveViability probes for every raise legal move (~10K probes/game). Actions with params ARE always-complete if their effect tree has no decision effects (chooseOne/chooseN/rollRandom). Removing the params.length > 0 exclusion saved 10.61%. LESSON: overly conservative exclusion logic can create massive hidden costs in hot paths.

Passing a profiler object through the kernel hot path caused 34% overhead — NOT from the profiling calls themselves but from V8 JIT deoptimization. The profiler field on EffectContext created polymorphic object shapes that prevented V8 from inlining and optimizing hot path functions. Stripping the profiler from kernel options in the simulator (keeping only sim-level timing) saved 23.94%. CRITICAL LESSON: never thread optional diagnostic objects through hot-path function signatures. Keep instrumentation at the boundary, not throughout the call graph.

buildIntRangeCandidates built full 960-element arrays for raise amount ranges that were immediately downsampled to 10. Direct mathematical computation of the downsampled subset (O(maxResults * required) vs O(range)) eliminated 41.8% of legalMoves time. Pattern: when selecting a small subset from a large range, compute the subset directly instead of materializing the full range.

evalValue concat handler used .map() + .filter() + .map().join() = 3 intermediate arrays per call. With 100K+ concat evals, this was 300K+ unnecessary array allocations (41% of CPU). Replacing with single-pass for loop + string concatenation saved 27.95%. ALSO: aggregate handler used .map() with per-item { ...ctx, bindings: { ...ctx.bindings, [bind]: item } } = 2 spreads per item. Replacing with mutable bindings object (safe for synchronous evalValue) eliminated all per-item spreads. CRITICAL LESSON: .map().filter().reduce() chains in hot paths are allocation bombs. Always use for loops in performance-critical code.

resolveBindingTemplate ran a regex /\{([^{}]+)\}/g on EVERY call. In Texas Hold'em, 100% of templates are plain strings with no {binding} placeholders. A single indexOf('{') === -1 check eliminated 20.54% of total runtime. CRITICAL LESSON: regex-based string processing in hot paths MUST have a fast-path for the common case (no matches). This will also benefit FITL if many of its template strings are plain.

Inlining logic into evalValue (exp-014: switch dispatch + inline validation) caused V8 JIT deoptimization — the function became too large for inlining. Lazy closures (exp-015) were MORE expensive than objects. V8 is extremely sensitive to function size in the 100K+ call-per-game range. LESSON: for ultra-hot functions like evalValue (~2M calls/game), do NOT make the function body larger. Keep it small and delegate to helper functions that V8 can inline individually.

After interpreter optimizations (mutable context, concat/aggregate loops, binding template short-circuit, profiler removal), the compiled lifecycle effect path became 12-16% SLOWER than the interpreter. The compiled path's per-fragment overhead (normalizeFragmentResult, context spreading) exceeds the interpreter's optimized per-effect overhead (mutable workCtx, direct dispatch). LESSON: compiled effect paths need to be re-benchmarked after interpreter optimizations — they may no longer provide a net benefit.

In resolveRef, 'binding' refs are the most common type in effect chains but were handled last in a 14-way if-else chain. Moving the binding check to the first position eliminated ~700K unnecessary string comparisons per game, saving 3.51%. Pattern: profile which discriminant is most frequent in hot-path dispatch chains and put it first.

Nested effect scopes (if/let/forEach calling applyEffectsWithBudgetState) re-created mutable state clones + DraftTracker Sets even though the parent scope already had a mutable state. Checking cursor.tracker to skip redundant createMutableState/createDraftTracker saved 8%. Pattern: any scope-boundary setup that is idempotent should check if the parent already did it.

createSeatResolutionContext rebuilds 5 Maps + arrays from scratch on every call. For FITL with 30K+ probeMoveViability calls per benchmark, this was the #1 redundant computation. A WeakMap cache keyed on def.seats (stable reference) with playerCount validation eliminated 6.31% of runtime. Pattern: any function that builds Maps/Sets from GameDef fields and is called per-move or per-probe should use WeakMap caching.

Adding ANY new field to EffectCursor or GameDefRuntime interfaces causes V8 hidden class deoptimization (4-7% regression). Even optional fields that are always present within a scope break monomorphism. This was proven across 5 experiments (exp-003: pipelineActionIds on GameDefRuntime, exp-006: triggerIndex on GameDefRuntime, exp-013: arena on EffectCursor). CRITICAL: never add properties to hot-path object interfaces for optimization purposes.

Import aliasing (import { X as Y }) in hot-path modules causes V8 JIT deoptimization (6.82% regression). V8 inlines based on exact function identity; aliased imports change the identity at the call site, breaking monomorphic dispatch. Never rename exports via aliased imports in performance-critical modules.

Branch reordering for early-exit optimization (moving phase check before contract evaluation) caused 4% regression in FITL. V8 JIT optimizes branch prediction based on the ORIGINAL ordering profile. Also, for...in iteration is consistently slower than Set-based iteration in V8 (4.2% regression). V8 prefers typed collection iteration over property enumeration.

advanceToDecisionPoint calls legalMoves(earlyExitAfterFirst) in a loop. Checking trivial actions (no params, no pre, always-complete) FIRST when earlyExitAfterFirst eliminates O(actions) per iteration. For FITL: 41% improvement.

V8 hidden class deoptimization extends to ALL frequently-created objects in the kernel hot path (MoveEnumerationState, EffectCursor, GameDefRuntime, ReadContext). Adding ANY field causes 2-7% regression. Also: WeakMap/Map caching, frozen array iteration, and closure variable capture all cause V8 deopt in this codebase.

96% of FITL legalMoves cost is ENUMERATION (action preflights + pipeline predicate evaluation + decision sequence admission), only 4% is classification probes. Sim-level profiling attributed this to legalMoves total, hiding that the bottleneck is inside the enumeration effect execution calls, not the classification step.

Probes (probeMoveViability) are CHEAPER than agent completions (evaluatePlayableMoveCandidate). Probe stops at first decision (~1ms), completion resolves ALL decisions (~5-10ms). Never bypass probes to shift work to agents — 5-10x cost increase per move.

resolvePlayerSel called listPlayers + sortAndDedupePlayers for 'actor' and 'active' selectors that always resolve to a single known player ID. Fast-pathing to return [playerId] directly avoids listPlayers array allocation, Set creation, array spread, and sort — 4 allocations per call. With 15K+ calls per benchmark, saved 1.92%. Pattern: any selector resolving to a single known value should skip collection infrastructure.

Early exits in CALLEE functions work for hot-path optimization, even when modifying the CALLER's control flow fails. evaluateActionSelectorContracts had an early-exit check before Set creation that saved 1.11%. The key insight: V8 JIT optimizes the caller based on its branch structure. Changes inside callees don't disrupt the caller's JIT optimization. Modifying hot-path caller control flow causes V8 deopt, but modifying callee return paths does not.

WeakSet.has() as an 'already validated' cache is profitable when the validation cost exceeds the hash lookup cost AND the validation callee is in the PREFLIGHT path (not the kernel execution path). assertEvalRuntimeResourcesContract validated the same object 25 times per legalMoves call. A single WeakSet.has() check saved 1.48%. Contrasts with exp-012 where WeakSet on GameState inside legalChoicesDiscover caused +12.9% regression. Pattern: use WeakSet for idempotent validation caching in PREFLIGHT callees only.

Agent behavior changes (completionsPerTemplate, decision parameters) are NOT safe performance optimizations. Different move selections produce different game state trajectories with different legalMoves complexity. In FITL, reducing completions from 3 to 2 made the agent 27% faster but caused 16% slower legalMoves due to harder game states. Also: adding cross-module imports between agents/ and kernel/ hot-path modules causes catastrophic V8 deoptimization (13% regression from a single import).

Kernel computation function modifications are UNIFORMLY unsafe: direct construction replacing spread (exp-010: +8%), spread removal (exp-011: +11%), early exits in computation callees (exp-002: +2%, exp-007: +6%). V8 JIT has deeply profiled the kernel's object construction and iteration patterns. The ONLY safe modifications are to VALIDATION callees that perform no actual computation. ANY change to computation callees or construction patterns triggers hidden class polymorphism and JIT deopt.
